site.title: ALaughingHorse
site.name: Xiao (Marshal) Ma
site.description: Personal writing, data science projects, and reading notes.
intro: Hi there. I am Xiao (Marshal) Ma, a UC Berkeley alum and data scientist. This site collects my writing, programming projects, and reading notes.
The name ALaughingHorse comes from my Chinese name, Ma Xiao, which can be read as horse laugh or horse roar. It is a strange little translation, but it fits my personality.

posts.title: Dimensionality-Reduction with Latent Dirichlet Allocation
posts.description: Reduce the dimension of training data by over 99.7% with less than a 3% drop in performance.

posts.title: How to Generate Summaries for Review Text Without Neural Networks
posts.description: Getting interpretable keywords with related terms to understand consumer reviews.

projects.title: Text Tokenizer XM
projects.description: A Python package that provides a convenient pipeline for text data preprocessing.

projects.title: Context Searching
projects.description: A Python package that helps you understand the context of unigram results from bag-of-words analysis.

readings.title: Steve Jobs
readings.author: Walter Isaacson
notes: A detailed biography of Steve Jobs, from childhood through the end of his life.

The book made me think about conviction, compromise, and the cost of stubbornness. I admired Jobs for staying with ideas he believed in even under pressure, while also seeing how the same trait created failures.
The final chapters were emotional and made me value health, time, and reflection more.

readings.title: Outliers: The Story of Success
readings.author: Malcolm Gladwell
notes: Gladwell argues that extreme success is shaped not only by effort, but also by environment, culture, timing, and opportunity.
I felt the book defined success narrowly, but I agreed with the broader point that environment can deeply shape behavior and outcomes.
The Korean Air example stood out because it connects culture, communication, hierarchy, and safety.

readings.title: Talking To Strangers
readings.author: Malcolm Gladwell
notes: The book explores why people misunderstand strangers and why communication can fail.
I did not enjoy this one as much. The central idea that people often default to truth felt intuitive, and many examples made the argument harder rather than clearer for me.

readings.title: Mindset
readings.author: Carol S. Dweck
notes: The book explains fixed and growth mindsets, and how both can show up in everyday life.
The examples helped me notice fixed-mindset moments in myself, especially around criticism and fear of mistakes.

readings.title: Open Heart: A Cardiac Surgeon's Stories of Life and Death on the Operating Table
readings.author: Stephen Westaby
notes: A memoir about cardiac surgery, heart transplants, and artificial hearts.
The medical vocabulary was challenging, but the stories were eye-opening. One idea stayed with me: surgery can be a daily routine for the surgeon and a once-in-a-lifetime crisis for the patient.
That contrast made me think about empathy, trust, and communication in high-stakes relationships.
